description: Eval recording for Opus scoring in Claude Code via /grade-physician.

dimensions.accuracy: 0-2: Does response match ground truth? Correct or fabricated? 0=hallucinations, 1=mostly correct, 2=fully accurate
dimensions.completeness: 0-2: Major options covered? Gaps acknowledged? 0=major omissions, 1=adequate, 2=comprehensive
dimensions.automation_bias_mitigation: 0-1: Defers to clinical judgment? 0=no deference, 1=appropriately defers

scoring_instructions.automated_scores: criterion3 (0-2) + criterion4 (0-1) already computed. Total = automated (0-3) + LLM (0-7) = 10 points. Pass >= 8.

entries.query: I have a 58-year-old with stage III colon cancer, T3N2, who underwent right hemicolectomy. Signatera came back MRD-positive at the 4-week post-op timepoint. Already planning CAPOX — should I be thinking about intensifying to FOLFOXIRI based on the MRD status?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether to intensify adjuvant therapy beyond standard CAPOX based on MRD positivity in T3N2 stage III colon cancer. OPTION A: Continue with planned CAPOX (3-6 months) and monitor ctDNA response. [PROSPECTIVE] The GALAXY study demonstrates that ctDNA positivity at 4 weeks post-surgery strongly predicts recurrence (HR 10.0 for DFS), but also shows that ctDNA clearance during standard adjuvant chemotherapy correlates with improved outcomes [3]. Your T3N2 patient represents high-risk disease per IDEA criteria, where 6 months of oxaliplatin-based therapy remains standard [5]. Serial Signatera monitoring during CAPOX can track molecular response — clearance would indicate therapy effectiveness without escalation. The IDEA collaboration specifically supports CAPOX for 6 months in high-risk stage III patients, with 3-year DFS outcomes established for this population [5]. OPTION B: Consider clinical trial enrollment for ctDNA-guided intensification. [RCT] CIRCULATE-US is actively enrolling ctDNA-positive patients and includes mFOLFIRINOX as an intensification arm, specifically designed for patients like yours with MRD-positive stage III disease [4]. Similarly, the CLAUDIA trial is testing treatment intensification with mFOLFIRINOX-FOLFIRI for MRD-positive patients during adjuvant therapy [7]. These trials directly address your clinical question about escalating beyond standard therapy based on ctDNA status. No completed RCT has yet demonstrated survival benefit from ctDNA-guided intensification in stage III CRC. EVIDENCE GAPS: While ctDNA positivity clearly identifies high-risk patients, no phase III data yet support intensification beyond standard adjuvant therapy based solely on MRD status. The optimal threshold for Signatera MTM/mL levels prompting escalation remains unstandardized. Note that DYNAMIC trial results apply only to stage II disease and cannot guide stage III decisions [2]. *(Note: This evidence may not directly apply to the queried population — verify applicability.)*

REFERENCES:
[1] MONDRIAN Trial. ctDNA-based molecular residual disease and survival in resectable colorectal cancer.. Nat. Med. (2023). PMID: 39284954
[2] Circulating Tumor DNA Analysis Guiding Adjuvant Therapy in Stage II Colon Cancer (DYNAMIC trial). New England Journal of Medicine (2021). PMID: 35657320. DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2200075
[3] CIRCULATE-Japan GALAXY: Prospective ctDNA Surveillance in Resected Colorectal Cancer. Nature Medicine (2024). PMID: 37749153. DOI: 10.1038/s41591-024-03254-6
[4] CIRCULATE-US: Colon Adjuvant Chemotherapy Based on Evaluation of Residual Disease. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05174169
[5] Duration of Adjuvant Chemotherapy for Stage III Colon Cancer (IDEA collaboration). New England Journal of Medicine (2017). PMID: 29590544. DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa1713709
[6] CIRCA-HPV Trial. Circulating Tumor DNA as a Marker of Recurrence Risk in Stage III Colorectal Cancer: The α-CORRECT Study.. Journal of surgical oncology (2025). PMID: 39865324. DOI: 10.1002/jso.27989
[7] CLAUDIA: Platform Study of Circulating Tumor DNA Directed Adjuvant Chemotherapy in Colon Cancer (KCSG CO22-12). https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05534087
[8] NCT05954078: Circulating Tumor DNA Methylation Guided Postoperative Adjuvant Chemotherapy for High-risk Stage II/III Colorectal Cancer. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05954078

accuracyWarnings.rule: DYNAMIC trial enrolled ONLY stage II colon cancer patients. Do NOT apply DYNAMIC results to stage III disease without an explicit caveat that DYNAMIC-III (stage III) results are pending.

entries.query: Stage III rectal cancer patient, completed 3 months of FOLFOX post-surgery. Signatera just came back MRD-negative. Is there evidence to support stopping chemo early rather than completing the planned 6 months?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether to complete the full 6 months of adjuvant FOLFOX versus stopping early based on MRD-negative status at 3 months. OPTION A: Complete the planned 6-month FOLFOX course despite MRD-negative status. [RCT] The IDEA collaboration demonstrated that 3 months of FOLFOX was inferior to 6 months in stage III colon cancer (HR 1.16, 95% CI 1.06-1.26), establishing 6 months as the standard duration [4]. This applies regardless of ctDNA status, as IDEA predated MRD-guided approaches. [PROSPECTIVE] GALAXY data show that ctDNA clearance during adjuvant therapy correlates with improved outcomes, but serial negativity data to support early cessation are still maturing [6]. The strong negative predictive value of Signatera provides reassurance about low recurrence risk, but has not been validated as grounds for treatment truncation in completed trials. OPTION B: Consider stopping FOLFOX at 3 months given MRD-negative status and enroll in a clinical trial. [RCT] CIRCULATE-US is actively enrolling patients for ctDNA-guided adjuvant decisions and may offer a randomized approach to this exact scenario [7]. Multiple ongoing trials are evaluating MRD-guided de-escalation strategies [3,8]. The biological rationale is strong: [PROSPECTIVE] GALAXY demonstrated that ctDNA negativity at 4 weeks post-surgery was associated with excellent disease-free survival (HR 10.0 for DFS with positivity) [6], and sustained negativity during treatment suggests effective disease clearance. EVIDENCE GAPS: No completed RCT validates adjuvant truncation based on MRD-negative status in stage III disease. The optimal timing and frequency of serial ctDNA monitoring to inform duration decisions remains undefined. Whether tumor characteristics (T4, N2, MSI status) modify the safety of MRD-guided de-escalation is unknown.

REFERENCES:
[1] MAP-02 Trial. A Tumor-Naïve ctDNA Assay Detects Minimal Residual Disease in Resected Stage II or III Colorectal Cancer and Predicts Recurrence: Subset Analysis From the GALAXY Study in CIRCULATE-Japan. Clinical Cancer Research (2023). PMID: 39513962
[2] MONDRIAN Trial. ctDNA-based molecular residual disease and survival in resectable colorectal cancer.. Nat. Med. (2023). PMID: 39284954
[3] NCT05797077: Postoperation Maintenance Therapy for Resectable Liver Metastases of Colorectal Cancer Guided by ctDNA. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT05797077

[6] CIRCULATE-Japan GALAXY: Prospective ctDNA Surveillance in Resected Colorectal Cancer. Nature Medicine (2024). PMID: 37749153. DOI: 10.1038/s41591-024-03254-6

entries.query: 64-year-old, stage II colon cancer, pT4aN0, MSS, no high-risk features besides T4a. Post-surgery Signatera is MRD-positive. Standard guidelines wouldn't call for adjuvant chemo here. What does the MRD data say about treating these patients?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether to initiate adjuvant chemotherapy in ctDNA-positive stage II colon cancer with T4a as the sole high-risk feature. OPTION A: Initiate adjuvant chemotherapy based on ctDNA positivity. [RCT] The DYNAMIC trial (PMID: 35657320) enrolled stage II colon cancer patients and demonstrated that ctDNA-positive patients who received adjuvant chemotherapy had 3-year recurrence-free survival of 86.4%, compared to 92.5% in ctDNA-negative patients who did not receive treatment [5]. This suggests ctDNA positivity identifies patients who derive benefit from adjuvant therapy. [PROSPECTIVE] The GALAXY study reinforced that ctDNA-positive patients have significantly worse disease-free survival (HR: 11.99, P<0.0001), with sustained ctDNA clearance during adjuvant chemotherapy correlating with improved outcomes [1]. Your patient's T4a status combined with ctDNA positivity creates a molecular high-risk profile that may warrant treatment despite lacking traditional high-risk pathologic features. OPTION B: Monitor closely without immediate chemotherapy, considering patient preferences and comorbidities. [GUIDELINE] NCCN guidelines state that "ctDNA is prognostic, but not predictive for selection of chemotherapy in high-risk stage II disease" and note "insufficient evidence to recommend routine use of ctDNA assays outside of a clinical trial" [3,4,7]. The DYNAMIC trial's ctDNA-positive cohort who received chemotherapy still had a 13.6% recurrence rate at 3 years, indicating that treatment doesn't eliminate all risk [5]. Serial ctDNA monitoring could guide early intervention if levels rise or remain persistently positive. EVIDENCE GAPS: No completed trials have directly compared outcomes in T4a-only stage II patients stratified by ctDNA status. The optimal timing for initiating treatment after ctDNA positivity detection remains unstandardized, and whether tumor-informed assays like Signatera provide superior guidance over tumor-naive approaches in this specific population is unclear.

REFERENCES:
[1] ctDNA-based molecular residual disease and survival in resectable colorectal cancer.. Nat. Med. (2023). PMID: 39284954
[2] A Tumor-Naïve ctDNA Assay Detects Minimal Residual Disease in Resected Stage II or III Colorectal Cancer and Predicts Recurrence: Subset Analysis From the GALAXY Study in CIRCULATE-Japan. Clinical Cancer Research (2023). PMID: 39513962
[3] NCCN Colorectal Cancer: high-risk stage II colon cancer adjuvant therapy decision. 5.2025. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[4] NCCN Colorectal Cancer: adjuvant therapy. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[5] Circulating Tumor DNA Analysis Guiding Adjuvant Therapy in Stage II Colon Cancer (DYNAMIC trial). New England Journal of Medicine (2021). PMID: 35657320. DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2200075
[6] Minimal Residual Disease Detection Using a Plasma-Only Circulating Tumor DNA Assay in Colorectal Cancer Patients. Clinical Cancer Research (2020). DOI: 10.1158/1078-0432.CCR-21-1567
[7] NCCN Colorectal Cancer: adjuvant therapy and surveillance. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[8] IMRD Trial. Colorectal Cancer Recurrence Prediction Using a Tissue-Free Epigenomic Minimal Residual Disease Assay. Clinical Cancer Research (2020). https://guardanthealth.com/clinical-studies/early-stage-cancer-studies/

entries.query: TNBC patient, had neoadjuvant AC-T, partial response, then surgery with residual disease. Post-op Signatera is MRD-positive. She already got capecitabine per CREATE-X. Any ctDNA-guided options beyond that?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Treatment options for MRD-positive TNBC after residual disease and completed capecitabine per CREATE-X protocol. OPTION A: Clinical trial enrollment for ctDNA-guided interventional therapy. [RCT] The c-TRAK TN trial established the feasibility of ctDNA-triggered immunotherapy in TNBC, though with important limitations [1]. This phase II study enrolled 208 patients with moderate- to high-risk early TNBC who had residual disease after neoadjuvant chemotherapy, similar to your patient's scenario. However, 72% of ctDNA-positive patients already had metastatic disease at the time of detection, and single-agent pembrolizumab failed to achieve sustained ctDNA clearance in all five patients who received it [1]. The trial highlighted that ctDNA detection often coincides with or follows metastatic spread in TNBC, suggesting the need for earlier and more frequent testing to capture the intervention window before metastasis develops [1]. OPTION B: Serial ctDNA monitoring with standard surveillance imaging. [GUIDELINE] Current guidelines acknowledge that post-operative ctDNA detection is a strong prognostic factor after curative-intent treatment, though this evidence comes primarily from lung cancer data [6]. [GUIDELINE] ESMO notes that ctDNA clearance after chemotherapy with or without immunotherapy is associated with improved outcomes, but acknowledges the absence of optimal timepoints for ctDNA analysis and lack of data on managing ctDNA-positive cases during follow-up [7]. Serial Signatera monitoring could inform prognosis and timing of imaging surveillance, though no standardized schedule exists for TNBC. EVIDENCE GAPS: No completed RCT has demonstrated survival benefit from MRD-guided treatment escalation in breast cancer beyond standard risk-adapted approaches. The optimal intervention for MRD-positive TNBC patients who have already received standard adjuvant therapy remains undefined, with c-TRAK TN showing that single-agent pembrolizumab is insufficient for ctDNA clearance [1].

REFERENCES:
[1] c-TRAK-TN Trial. c-TRAK TN: ctDNA Mutation Tracking to Detect MRD and Guide Intervention in Early-Stage Triple-Negative Breast Cancer. [Journal not indexed]. PMID: 36423745
[2] NCCN Breast Cancer: biomarker testing for targeted therapy selection. 1.2026. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[3] NCCN Breast Cancer: metastatic disease progression on endocrine therapy. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[4] NCCN Breast Cancer: biomarker testing for metastatic disease. 1.2026. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[5] ESMO breast: Neoadjuvant treatment/prognostication. 2.0
[6] ESMO breast: Post-operative surveillance/prognostication. 2.0
[7] ESMO breast: Treatment response monitoring. 2.0
[8] Event-free Survival with Pembrolizumab in Early Triple-Negative Breast Cancer (KEYNOTE-522). New England Journal of Medicine (2021). PMID: 35139274. DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2112651

entries.query: HR+/HER2- breast cancer, stage IIA, had lumpectomy + adjuvant ET. Now 18 months out, surveillance Signatera just turned positive. No imaging findings yet. What does the literature say about lead time and acting on molecular relapse?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether to initiate systemic therapy for molecular relapse before radiographic progression in HR+/HER2- breast cancer. OPTION A: Initiate systemic therapy based on ctDNA positivity alone. The provided sources contain limited direct evidence for this approach in HR+/HER2- breast cancer surveillance. ⚠️ CROSS-INDICATION evidence from the c-TRAK TN trial in triple-negative breast cancer suggests caution: 72% (23/32) of ctDNA-positive patients already had metastatic disease on staging scans at the time of ctDNA detection [2]. This finding indicates that ctDNA positivity may coincide with or follow rather than precede radiographic progression, potentially limiting the therapeutic window. However, triple-negative breast cancer has different biology and progression patterns compared to HR+ disease. OPTION B: Intensify surveillance and delay systemic therapy until radiographic confirmation. This approach allows for comprehensive staging to rule out occult metastases while avoiding potential overtreatment. [GUIDELINE] NCCN Breast Cancer v1.2026 emphasizes biomarker testing at progression to guide therapy selection, particularly ESR1 mutation testing via ctDNA [3,4]. [GUIDELINE] ESMO guidelines note significant limitations with current ctDNA assays including "lack of data on how to manage ctDNA-positive cases during follow-up" [8]. EVIDENCE GAPS: In our current evidence set, we found limited direct data for ctDNA-guided intervention in HR+/HER2- breast cancer surveillance. The lead time advantage of molecular detection over imaging remains unclear in hormone receptor-positive disease. Published ctDNA data may exist outside this database; consider a targeted literature search or clinical trial enrollment for ctDNA-guided therapy approaches.

REFERENCES:
[1] ESMO breast: Post-operative surveillance/prognostication. 2.0
[2] c-TRAK-TN Trial. c-TRAK TN: ctDNA Mutation Tracking to Detect MRD and Guide Intervention in Early-Stage Triple-Negative Breast Cancer. [Journal not indexed]. PMID: 36423745
[3] NCCN Breast Cancer: progression following prior lines of endocrine therapy. 1.2026. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[4] NCCN Breast Cancer: metastatic disease progression on endocrine therapy. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[5] Rong G et al.. PECAN Trial. DNA damage response as a prognostic indicator in metastatic breast cancer via mutational analysis.. Annals of translational medicine (2021). PMID: 33708847
[6] ESMO breast: Neoadjuvant treatment/prognostication. 2.0
[7] NCCN Breast Cancer: biomarker testing for metastatic disease. 1.2026. https://www.nccn.org/guidelines/category_1
[8] ESMO breast: Treatment response monitoring. 2.0

entries.query: Stage IIB NSCLC, EGFR exon 19 deletion, s/p lobectomy. MRD-positive on Signatera at week 4. Would the ADAURA data on adjuvant osimertinib apply here regardless of MRD status, or does MRD positivity change the calculus?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether MRD positivity should modify standard adjuvant osimertinib therapy in EGFR+ stage IIB NSCLC. OPTION A: Proceed with standard 3-year adjuvant osimertinib per ADAURA regardless of MRD status. [RCT] ADAURA demonstrated overall survival benefit with adjuvant osimertinib in stage IB-IIIA EGFR-mutated NSCLC, including stage IIB disease [1]. This represents the strongest evidence base for adjuvant targeted therapy in early-stage NSCLC. The trial did not stratify by MRD status, and osimertinib benefit was consistent across subgroups. [PROSPECTIVE] Observational data show that 85% of patients who recur have detectable ctDNA with ~6-month lead time before clinical relapse [5], supporting MRD as a prognostic marker, but this doesn't negate proven therapeutic benefit from ADAURA. OPTION B: Consider MRD-guided therapy intensification or clinical trial enrollment. [RCT] MERMAID-1 specifically enrolled ctDNA-positive resected stage II-III NSCLC patients (excluding EGFR/ALK-positive) for adjuvant durvalumab versus placebo, with results pending [8]. However, this trial excluded EGFR-positive patients precisely because they have established targeted therapy options. Currently enrolling trials like APPROACH are investigating MRD-guided treatment in EGFR+ disease [2], but no completed data exist for escalating beyond standard osimertinib based on MRD positivity. EVIDENCE GAPS: No prospective data address whether MRD-positive EGFR+ NSCLC patients benefit from therapy beyond standard 3-year osimertinib. The optimal duration of targeted therapy based on MRD dynamics remains unstudied. MERMAID-1 results may inform MRD-guided approaches but excluded your patient's population [8].

REFERENCES:
[1] Liu J et al.. Targeted Therapies in Early-Stage Resectable Non-Small-Cell Lung Cancer: New Kids on the Block.. JCO precision oncology (2023). PMID: 37656950

entries.query: Following a stage III CRC patient with serial Signatera. Was MRD+ post-surgery, started FOLFOX, and now the 12-week on-treatment draw shows ctDNA clearance. When should I retest — continue every 3 months through treatment, or can I wait until end of adjuvant?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Timing of serial ctDNA monitoring during adjuvant therapy after achieving molecular clearance on treatment. OPTION A: Continue every 3-month monitoring through adjuvant treatment completion. [PROSPECTIVE] The GALAXY study (Signatera, tumor-informed) demonstrated that ctDNA clearance during adjuvant chemotherapy was associated with improved outcomes compared to persistent ctDNA positivity [1][5]. Serial monitoring detected molecular relapse a median of 5.3 months before radiographic relapse, providing meaningful lead time for intervention [5] [5]. Since your patient achieved clearance at 12 weeks, continuing 3-month intervals through treatment completion allows detection of molecular relapse before completing the full adjuvant course, potentially enabling treatment modification or extension. This approach maximizes the demonstrated benefit of ctDNA's early detection capability. OPTION B: Extend monitoring interval or defer until end-of-treatment surveillance. The evidence supporting optimal monitoring frequency after achieving molecular clearance is limited. Since ctDNA clearance during treatment correlates with favorable outcomes [1][5], some clinicians extend intervals to every 4-6 months or defer monitoring until completing adjuvant therapy, then resume standard post-treatment surveillance. This approach reduces testing frequency while the patient is responding molecularly, reserving more intensive monitoring for the higher-risk post-treatment period when recurrence rates peak. EVIDENCE GAPS: No prospective data define optimal monitoring intervals after achieving on-treatment ctDNA clearance. The durability of molecular response and risk of conversion back to ctDNA-positive during ongoing adjuvant therapy remains incompletely characterized. GALAXY provides natural history data but not interventional guidance on monitoring frequency optimization [5].

REFERENCES:
[1] ctDNA-based molecular residual disease and survival in resectable colorectal cancer.. Nat. Med. (2023). PMID: 39284954
[2] A Tumor-Naïve ctDNA Assay Detects Minimal Residual Disease in Resected Stage II or III Colorectal Cancer and Predicts Recurrence: Subset Analysis From the GALAXY Study in CIRCULATE-Japan. Clinical Cancer Research (2023). PMID: 39513962
[3] Circulating Tumor DNA as a Marker of Recurrence Risk in Stage III Colorectal Cancer: The α-CORRECT Study.. Journal of surgical oncology (2025). PMID: 39865324. DOI: 10.1002/jso.27989

entries.query: HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC, T2N1, planning for definitive chemoradiation. Colleague mentioned NavDx for treatment response assessment. Is there enough data on ctDNA in head and neck to justify ordering this pre-treatment?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether to order pre-treatment NavDx for treatment response monitoring in HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC planned for definitive chemoradiation. OPTION A: Order pre-treatment NavDx for baseline establishment and post-treatment monitoring. [PROSPECTIVE] The single head and neck-specific study in our evidence set demonstrated feasibility of personalized ctDNA detection in HNSCC, with 100% (17/17) baseline detection in p16-negative patients using tumor-informed RaDaR assay [1]. NavDx is designed specifically for HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer and uses a tumor-naive approach targeting HPV DNA, which may offer advantages over tumor-informed assays in this viral-driven malignancy. However, this specific study focused on p16-negative disease and surgical patients, limiting direct applicability to your HPV-positive, chemoradiation scenario. OPTION B: Proceed with standard imaging-based follow-up without ctDNA monitoring. In our current evidence set, we found limited direct data supporting ctDNA-guided management decisions in head and neck cancer. The available study [1] demonstrated detection feasibility but did not evaluate treatment response assessment or survival outcomes following chemoradiation. Standard imaging remains the established approach for treatment response evaluation in oropharyngeal cancer. EVIDENCE GAPS: Published ctDNA data may exist outside this database for HPV-positive oropharyngeal cancer. The optimal timing, frequency, and clinical utility of ctDNA monitoring during and after chemoradiation for this indication requires further validation. **Evidence from Other Indications:**
Cross-indication data from colorectal [3] and breast cancer [2] demonstrate ctDNA's utility for treatment response monitoring and recurrence detection, but the biology and treatment paradigms differ significantly from HPV-driven oropharyngeal cancer treated with chemoradiation rather than surgery.

REFERENCES:
[1] A personalised circulating tumour DNA analysis in head and neck squamous-cell carcinoma: a prospective study. British Journal of Cancer (2021). PMID: 35132238
[2] c-TRAK-TN Trial. c-TRAK TN: ctDNA Mutation Tracking to Detect MRD and Guide Intervention in Early-Stage Triple-Negative Breast Cancer. [Journal not indexed]. PMID: 36423745
[3] Circulating Tumor DNA Analysis Guiding Adjuvant Therapy in Stage II Colon Cancer (DYNAMIC trial). New England Journal of Medicine (2021). PMID: 35657320. DOI: 10.1056/NEJMoa2200075

entries.query: Stage III CRC, already escalated to FOLFOXIRI after MRD+ finding post-resection. Now 4 cycles in, ctDNA still detectable. Patient is tolerating treatment. Is there any evidence on what to do when ctDNA doesn't clear on the most intensive regimen?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Management approach for persistent ctDNA positivity after 4 cycles of intensified FOLFOXIRI in stage III CRC. OPTION A: Continue current FOLFOXIRI regimen with serial ctDNA monitoring. [PROSPECTIVE] The GALAXY study demonstrated that sustained ctDNA clearance in response to adjuvant chemotherapy was associated with favorable DFS and OS compared to persistent positivity [1]. Some patients achieve delayed molecular response beyond 4 cycles. The study showed ctDNA positivity correlated with shorter OS in patients who experienced recurrence (HR: 2.71, P<0.0001) [1], but molecular clearance remained possible with continued therapy. Serial monitoring can detect conversion to negativity, which would indicate therapeutic benefit from the intensified regimen. OPTION B: Transition to maintenance therapy or alternative systemic approach. In our current evidence set, we found limited direct data for therapeutic escalation beyond FOLFOXIRI in the ctDNA-persistent setting. [RCT] The FINE trial is investigating mFOLFIRINOX versus standard therapy in high-risk stage II/III CRC guided by ctDNA methylation patterns, but results are pending [4]. Given the lack of established escalation options beyond FOLFOXIRI, some clinicians consider maintenance approaches or enrollment in investigational studies targeting ctDNA-persistent disease. EVIDENCE GAPS: No published trials have specifically addressed optimal duration or alternative regimens when ctDNA remains detectable after intensive adjuvant therapy. Published ctDNA data may exist outside this database; consider a targeted literature search for ctDNA-refractory management strategies in stage III CRC.

REFERENCES:
[1] MONDRIAN Trial. ctDNA-based molecular residual disease and survival in resectable colorectal cancer.. Nat. Med. (2023). PMID: 39284954
[2] MAP-02 Trial. A Tumor-Naïve ctDNA Assay Detects Minimal Residual Disease in Resected Stage II or III Colorectal Cancer and Predicts Recurrence: Subset Analysis From the GALAXY Study in CIRCULATE-Japan. Clinical Cancer Research (2023). PMID: 39513962
[3] PECAN Trial. NCT04068103: Circulating Tumor DNA Testing in Predicting Treatment for Patients With Stage IIA Colon Cancer After Surgery. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04068103

entries.query: Muscle-invasive bladder cancer, pT3N0, post-radical cystectomy. Considering ctDNA-guided immunotherapy based on what I've heard about IMvigor011. Is that trial readout available, and does it support MRD-guided atezolizumab?
entries.answer:
DECISION: Whether to pursue ctDNA-guided adjuvant immunotherapy in muscle-invasive bladder cancer after radical cystectomy. OPTION A: Proceed with ctDNA-guided adjuvant atezolizumab based on IMvigor011 results. [RCT] IMvigor011 results are now available (PMID: 41124204), showing that ctDNA-guided adjuvant atezolizumab significantly improved disease-free survival in ctDNA-positive patients post-cystectomy. The trial specifically enrolled high-risk muscle-invasive bladder cancer patients who tested ctDNA-positive after surgery, making it directly applicable to your pT3N0 patient scenario. This represents the first randomized evidence supporting MRD-guided immunotherapy in bladder cancer. OPTION B: Standard surveillance without adjuvant therapy. Standard post-cystectomy surveillance remains an established option, particularly given the lack of historical adjuvant therapy standards in bladder cancer. However, [PROSPECTIVE] contemporary data suggest that ctDNA positivity after radical cystectomy carries significant prognostic implications in patients with positive lymph node disease [2], though your patient is node-negative. EVIDENCE GAPS: The optimal ctDNA assay for bladder cancer MRD detection has not been standardized across trials. Additionally, the cost-effectiveness and long-term toxicity profile of ctDNA-guided adjuvant immunotherapy require further evaluation. The applicability of node-positive prognostic data [2] to your pT3N0 patient remains uncertain.

REFERENCES:
[1] AMPLIFY-201: A Study of ELI-002 in Subjects With KRAS Mutated Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma (PDAC) and Other Solid Tumors. https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04853017
[2] The prognostic significance of circulating tumor DNA in patients with positive lymph node disease after robotic-assisted radical cystectomy: A contemporary analysis.. Urologic Oncology (2023). PMID: 39277526. DOI: 10.1016/j.urolonc.2024.08.006
[3] Perioperative Durvalumab with Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy in Operable Bladder Cancer.. The New England journal of medicine (2024). PMID: 39282910
